ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers rice noodles broadly; no brown-rice-specific vermicelli subcode identified. Approximate match used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of cooked brown rice beehoon is approximately 150g, equivalent to one medium bowl of noodles as commonly served in a Singaporean meal.

nutrient_source.title: Cooked brown rice beehoon — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for cooked brown rice beehoon (CR ID: F05070008). All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record. Note: the energy value of 369 kcal/100g and carbohydrate of 75.8g/100g are consistent with dry/uncooked weight basis for rice noodles; the record name states 'cooked' but the macros suggest dry-weight data. Values are preserved as reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Brown Rice Noodles / Rice Vermicelli — University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for 'cooked brown rice beehoon' was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 (low) is an analogous estimate based on published GI values for brown rice noodles and rice vermicelli in the University of Sydney GI Database, which report GI values in the range 53–61 for similar wholegrain rice noodle products. GL of 41.7 is calculated from the HPB-reported carbohydrate value of 75.8g per 100g and a 55g serving reference; note this GL is high due to the likely dry-weight carbohydrate basis. Confidence: 0.6. Input GI data flagged as 'Embedded GI Database' has been re-anchored to the University of Sydney GI Database as the approved GI source.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (41 mg/100g) and high dietary fibre (3.7g/100g) supporting arterial health
hypertension.guidance: Brown rice beehoon is suitable for individuals with hypertension due to its very low sodium content of 41 mg per 100g and 3.7g dietary fibre, which supports vascular health; however, patients should avoid high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or oyster sauce when preparing dishes with this noodle.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (55) with high dietary fibre (3.7g/100g) from whole-grain brown rice origin moderating glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Brown rice beehoon has a low GI of approximately 55 and provides 3.7g dietary fibre per 100g from its whole-grain origin, which moderates postprandial glucose rise compared to white rice noodles; individuals with type 2 diabetes should control portion size carefully, noting that the reported 75.8g carbohydrate per 100g likely reflects dry-weight data and cooked portions will have substantially lower carbohydrate density.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Whole-grain source with 3.7g dietary fibre, low sodium, zero cholesterol, and low saturated fat profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Brown rice beehoon is a whole-grain noodle with zero cholesterol (0 mg/100g), low sodium (41 mg/100g), and 3.7g dietary fibre per 100g, making it a heart-friendly carbohydrate choice; patients with cardiovascular disease should pair it with lean proteins and vegetables and avoid high-fat or high-sodium sauces.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Whole-grain fibre supporting gut microbiome and immune regulation; no pro-inflammatory components
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Brown rice beehoon provides 3.7g dietary fibre per 100g from a whole-grain source supporting gut microbiome health relevant to immune regulation in rheumatoid arthritis; pair with omega-3-rich proteins and anti-inflammatory vegetables and avoid cooking with palm oil or lard.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cooked brown rice beehoon is a Singaporean wholegrain rice vermicelli noodle made from brown rice flour and water. It is light brown in colour with a soft, tender texture and a mild nutty flavour characteristic of whole-grain rice products. Unlike conventional white rice beehoon (bee hoon), brown rice beehoon retains the bran layer of the rice grain, providing higher dietary fibre (3.7g per 100g) and a lower glycaemic impact. It is commonly used in Singaporean home cooking and hawker-style dishes such as stir-fried beehoon, soup noodles, and dry noodle salads. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides approximately 369 kcal, 7.9g protein, 3.03g fat, 75.8g carbohydrate, 3.7g dietary fibre, and 41mg sodium — values consistent with a dry or minimally processed noodle product. The high carbohydrate and energy values suggest the HPB data reflects a dry-weight basis; cooked weight will yield approximately 25–30g carbohydrate per 100g. The low glycaemic index of approximately 55 and the whole-grain fibre content make it a preferred noodle choice for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. It is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and halal-suitable. Clinically, it is recommended over white rice noodles for glycaemic management and cardiovascular health due to its fibre content and whole-grain origin.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database (CR ID: F05070008) with confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar brown rice noodle products, assigned medium confidence (0.6). Overall record confidence is high given the primary HPB source for all macros and micronutrients.
notes: 1. The HPB-reported energy of 369 kcal/100g and carbohydrate of 75.8g/100g are consistent with dry or minimally processed noodle weight rather than fully cooked weight; the record name states 'cooked' but the macro profile suggests dry-weight data. Values are preserved as reported by HPB without adjustment. Users should note that cooked weight will yield approximately 25–30g carbohydrate per 100g. 2. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific brown rice beehoon product are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for F05070008 as of 2026. 3. sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; brown rice beehoon contains no added or free sugars. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; this is a plant-based grain product containing no cholesterol. 4. GI value of 55 is an analogous estimate anchored to the University of Sydney GI Database for brown rice noodle products; the original input cited 'Embedded GI Database' which is not an approved source and has been replaced. 5. Cultural classification corrected from 'western' (input error) to 'Singaporean'; brown rice beehoon is a Singaporean wholegrain noodle product. 6. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03311069 and FoodEx2 A00LC are approximate matches for rice noodles; no exact brown rice vermicelli subcode exists in either system as of 2026.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' to align with allowed values for G5.
Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' as GI 55 falls into the 'low' category (GI <= 55) as per common GI classifications and the VERITAS_SCORING_MODEL_v2.md implicit rule for GI categories.
Added the missing alias 'Shu Zao Mi Mi Fen' to the flat 'aliases' array to ensure consistency with 'aliases_by_language.romanized' and pass Q8.
